name: Prahlada
description: Prahlada is a significant figure in Hindu mythology, revered as the epitome of unwavering devotion (bhakti) to Vishnu. Born into a family of Asuras (demons), his father, Hiranyakashipu, was a powerful king who had earned a boon making him virtually invincible. Despite his father's tyrannical rule and vehement opposition to Vishnu worship, Prahlada remained steadfast in his faith. His devotion was not merely ritualistic but deeply internalized, manifesting in his compassion, humility, and unwavering belief in the omnipresence of Vishnu. Prahlada's story serves as a powerful allegory for the triumph of good over evil, the strength of faith in the face of adversity, and the divine protection afforded to true devotees. He is often depicted as a young boy, symbolizing purity and innocence, further emphasizing the profound nature of his devotion. His narratives are central to Vaishnava traditions, particularly in the context of the Narasimha avatar of Vishnu.
subtitle: The Steadfast Devotee

keyMyths.title: Hiranyakashipu's Wrath
keyMyths.description: Hiranyakashipu, enraged by his son's unwavering devotion to Vishnu, attempts to kill Prahlada through various means, including poisoning, throwing him off a cliff, and subjecting him to attacks by wild animals. Each attempt fails due to Vishnu's divine intervention and protection. This highlights the power of sincere devotion to overcome even the most formidable obstacles and the divine grace that shields the faithful.

keyMyths.title: Narasimha Avatar
keyMyths.description: Unable to kill Prahlada through conventional means due to his boon, Hiranyakashipu challenges Vishnu to appear in a pillar. Vishnu manifests as Narasimha, a half-man, half-lion avatar, at twilight (neither day nor night) on the threshold of his palace (neither inside nor outside), and kills Hiranyakashipu, fulfilling the conditions of the boon without violating them. This underscores the divine ingenuity and the absolute power of Vishnu to protect his devotees.

keyMyths.title: Prahlada's Kingdom
keyMyths.description: After the death of Hiranyakashipu, Prahlada is crowned king and rules with righteousness, compassion, and devotion to Vishnu. His reign is characterized by peace, prosperity, and the spread of Vaishnavism. This demonstrates the positive impact of righteous leadership and the transformative power of devotion on society.

sources.author: Winternitz, Maurice
sources.description: Provides an overview of the Puranic literature, including the Bhagavata Purana and Vishnu Purana, which contain the primary narratives of Prahlada.
sources.text: Provides an overview of the Puranic literature, including the Bhagavata Purana and Vishnu Purana, which contain the primary narratives of Prahlada.

sources.author: Doniger, Wendy
sources.description: Offers a comprehensive compilation of Hindu myths, including retellings of the Prahlada story and analyses of its symbolism and significance.
sources.text: Offers a comprehensive compilation of Hindu myths, including retellings of the Prahlada story and analyses of its symbolism and significance.

sources.author: Tagare, Ganesh Vasudeo
sources.description: A detailed translation and commentary on the Bhagavata Purana, providing insights into the philosophical and theological aspects of the Prahlada narratives.
sources.text: A detailed translation and commentary on the Bhagavata Purana, providing insights into the philosophical and theological aspects of the Prahlada narratives.
